'''Lucasfilm Ltd.''' or simply '''Lucasfilm (LFL)''', is an [[Wikipedia:United States|American]] [[Wikipedia:Film|film]] production company founded by [[George Lucas]] in [[1971]], based in [[Wikipedia:Marin County, California|Marin County, California]]. [[Kathleen Kennedy]] is currently its [[Wikipedia:President|president]]. It is a subsidiary of [[The Walt Disney Company]], following Disney's October [[2012]] purchase of Lucasfilm.

The company is best known for producing the ''[[Star Wars]]'' and ''[[Indiana Jones]]'' films, but it has produced other box-office hits. It has also been a leader in developing new film technology in [[Wikipedia:Special effects|special effects]], [[Wikipedia:Sound design|sound design]], [[Wikipedia:Computer animation|computer animation]], and various additional fields, through its subsidiaries that include [[Industrial Light & Magic]], [[Skywalker Sound]], and [[Lucasfilm Animation]]. Due to their expertise, its subsidiaries often assist in the production of non-Lucasfilm pictures.

==History==
[[File:LucasPanavision-MOSW.png|thumb|right|250px|George Lucas, the founder of Lucasfilm and creator of ''Star Wars''.]]

===The Original Trilogy===
Lucasfilm was founded as an independent production company by [[George Lucas]] in [[1971]] to maintain creative control over his films following his fraught experiences with Hollywood studios while producing [[Wikipedia:THX 1138|''THX 1138'']]. The company's first production was [[Wikipedia:American Graffiti|''American Graffiti'']], which was distributed by [[Wikipedia:Universal Pictures|Universal Pictures]] and grossed US$55 million in rentals.<ref name="Taylor">{{BookCite|author=Taylor, Chris|title=How Star Wars Conquered the Universe: The Past, Present, and Future of a Multibillion Dollar Franchise|publisher=[[Wikipedia:Basic Books|Basic Books]]|date=[[2014]]|isbn=978-0465089987}}</ref><ref name="Jones">{{BookCite|author=Jones, Brian Jay|title=George Lucas: A Life|publisher=[[Wikipedia:Little, Brown and Company|Little, Brown and Company]]|date=[[2016]]|isbn=978-0316257442}}</ref>

During the production of [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|''Star Wars'': Episode IV ''A New Hope'']], Lucasfilm established a special effects shop near [[Wikipedia:Van Nuys Airport|Van Nuys Airport]] in [[Wikipedia:Los Angeles|Los Angeles]], which later became the Lucasfilm subsidiary [[Industrial Light & Magic]] (ILM).<ref name="Jones" /> The first Star Wars film was released on [[May 25]], 1977 and became a box office success, grossing US$307,263,857.<ref name="A Day Long Remembered">{{WebCite|url=http://fromscripttodvd.com/star_wars_a_day_long_remembered.htm|text=May 25, 1977: A Day Long Remembered|author=Michael Coate|work=From Script to DVD.com|archivedate=20200304005712}}</ref><ref name="Heritage">{{WebCite|url=http://americanheritage.com/entertainment/articles/web/20060525-star-wars-george-lucas-movies-hollywood-luke-skywalker-darth-vader-american-graffiti-science-fiction-special-effects.shtml|text=How Star Wars Surprised the World|author=David Rapp|date=2006-05-25|work=[http://americanheritage.com AmericanHeritage.com]|archivedate=20060618153107}}</ref> In addition, Lucasfilm entered into merchandising agreements with several companies, including [[Wikipedia:Kenner Products|Kenner Products]], Image Factory, and [[Marvel Comics]] for Star Wars-related clothes, posters, toys, buttons, decals, and comics.<ref name="Jones" /> Using the money trickling from Lucasfilm's merchandising, Lucas established an office at a [[Wikipedia:Lankershim Boulevard|Lankershim Boulevard]] property called called "The Egg Company", with the help of the company's first CEO Charlie Weber.<ref name="Taylor" /><ref name="Jones" />

[[20th Century Studios|20th Century Fox]] was the original copyright owner of ''A New Hope''<ref name="Catalog">{{BookCite|title=Catalog of Copyright Entries - Motion Pictures (January-June 1977)|version=Third Series|number=Vol. 31, Pts. 12-13, N. 1|publisher=Copyright Office, [[Wikipedia:Library of Congress|Library of Congress]]|date=[[1977]]|issn=0090-8371|link=https://archive.org/details/1977motionpictur3311213libr/page/n98/mode/1up}}</ref> and the merchandising rights were split between them and Lucas 60/40. Lucasfilm gained all the ''Star Wars'' IP and licensing rights held by Fox, excluding ''A New Hope''{{'s}} copyright, on [[September 1]], [[1981]].<ref name="luckystars97">{{WebCite|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1997-02-06-fi-25849-story.html|text=By George, He Can Thank His Lucky 'Stars'|date=1997-02-06|work=[[Wikipedia:Los Angeles Times|Los Angeles Times]]|quote=Pollock negotiated for a 60-40 split of merchandising between Fox and Lucas to begin with; the percentage diminished over two years until all rights reverted to Lucas.|archivedate=20200807151245}}</ref><ref name="MakingOfRotJ">''[[The Making of Return of the Jedi]]''</ref><ref name="Copyright1">{{WebCite|url=https://cocatalog.loc.gov/cgi-bin/Pwebrecon.cgi?DB=local&PAGE=First&Search_Arg=Star+Wars+brochure+%26+132+other+titles.&Search_Code=TALL|text=Star Wars brochure & 132 other titles.|work=[[Wikipedia:Copyright Catalog|Copyright Catalog]]|archivedate=20210423103615}}</ref><ref name="Trademark1">{{WebCite|url=https://assignments.uspto.gov/assignments/q?db=tm&reel=0407&frame=0551|text=Trademark Assignment Details|work=[[Wikipedia:United States Patent and Trademark Office|U.S. Trademark Database]]|archivedate=20210423103918}}</ref><ref name="trademark2">{{WebCite|url=https://assignments.uspto.gov/assignments/q?db=tm&reel=0407&frame=F551|text=Trademark Assignment Details (Alderaan)|work=[[Wikipedia:United States Patent and Trademark Office|U.S. Trademark Database]]|archivedate=20210423104149}}</ref> ''A New Hope''{{'s}} copyright was eventually transferred to Lucasfilm on [[January 18]], [[1997]].<ref name="Copyright2">{{WebCite|url=https://cocatalog.loc.gov/cgi-bin/Pwebrecon.cgi?DB=local&PAGE=First&Search_Arg=Star+Wars+%26+1+other+title&Search_Code=TALL|text=Star Wars & 1 other title; By Twentieth Century Fox Film Corporation.|work=[[Wikipedia:Copyright Catalog|Copyright Catalog]]|archivedate=20210423104447}}</ref><ref name="Copyright3">{{WebCite|url=https://cocatalog.loc.gov/cgi-bin/Pwebrecon.cgi?DB=local&PAGE=First&Search_Arg=RE0000925302&Search_Code=REGS|text=Star wars / by Twentieth Century Fox Film Corporation. (Renewal)|work=[[Wikipedia:Copyright Catalog|Copyright Catalog]]|archivedate=20210423104624}}</ref>

In late 1977, Lucas established a subsidiary called "The Chapter II Company" to oversee production and funding of [[Star Wars: Episode V The Empire Strikes Back|''Star Wars'': Episode V ''The Empire Strikes Back'']]. In addition, Lucas relocated ILM from Los Angeles to [[Wikipedia:San Francisco|San Francisco]]. Lucas later purchased a ranch in [[Wikipedia:Marin County, California|Marin County]] near San Francisco which became [[Wikipedia:Skywalker Ranch|Skywalker Ranch]]. While working on ''The Empire Strikes Back'', Lucasfilm also produced a Star Wars spinoff film called ''[[The Star Wars Holiday Special]]'', which was distributed by [[Wikipedia:CBS|CBS]] on [[November 17]], [[1978]]. ''The Empire Strikes Back'' was released on [[May 21]], [[1980]], becoming a box office success and grossing nearly US$210 million during its first run.<ref name="Jones" />

Charlie Weber also served as the first CEO of Lucasfilm but left following the release of ''The Empire Strikes Back'' due to differences with Lucas. While Lucas wanted to focus on building Skywalker Ranch, making [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Star Wars'': Episode VI ''Return of the Jedi'']], and creating a filmmaker's community, Weber wanted Lucasfilm to diversify into other businesses. Lucas dismissed Weber and promoted [[Robert Greber]] as Lucasfilm's new CEO. Following the release of ''The Empire Strikes Back'', Lucasfilm closed down The Egg Company and relocated all its operations to Skywalker Ranch.<ref name="Jones" />

Lucasfilm produced the third Star Wars film ''Return of the Jedi'', which was released on [[May 25]], [[1983]] and grossed US$250 million by the end of the year. Besides ''Return of the Jedi'', Lucasfilm also produced [[Wikipedia:Raiders of the Lost Ark|''Raiders of the Lost Ark'']] and (1981) and [[Wikipedia:Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom|''Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom'']] (1984), which were distributed by [[Wikipedia:Paramount Studios|Paramount Studios]] and also performed well at the box office.<ref name="Jones" />

===Expansion and diversification===
[[File:ILMATST-SWFB.jpg|thumb|left|250px|Members of the Industrial Light & Magic team with an AT-ST walker model]]
During the 1980s, Lucas also opened ILM to producing special effects for non–Lucasfilm projects. The first of these non–Lucasfilm projects was the joint Paramount–[[The Walt Disney Company|Disney]] 1981 film [[Wikipedia:Dragonslayer (1981 film)|''Dragonslayer'']]. Other notable films that ILM worked on included [[Wikipedia:Star Trek II: The Wrath of Khan|''Star Trek II: The Wrath of Khan'']], [[Wikipedia:The Abyss|''The Abyss'']] (1989), [[Wikipedia:Terminator (franchise)|''Terminator'']], and [[Wikipedia:Jurassic Park|''Jurassic Park'']]. ILM became the preeminent special effects shop, winning the [[Wikipedia:Academy Awards|Academy Awards]] for Best Visual Effects thirteen times.<ref name="Jones" />

Using Star Wars merchandising revenue, Lucas continue to expand Skywalker Ranch. He envisioned Lucasfilm as a "one-stop, full service" company for other filmmakers. These services included providing writing retreats for screenwriters, editing equipment to film editors, and special effects services via ILM. During the 1980s, Lucas also invested in another Lucasfilm start-up called [[Wikipedia:THX|THX]], which focused on improving cinema theatres' sound quality using Lucas' [[THX]] sound system.<ref name="Jones" />

Lucas also established a computer game division at Lucasfilm and with the help of [[Edwin Catmull]] recruited [[Wikipedia:Peter Langston|Peter Langston]] to head that division. The [[LucasArts|Lucasfilm Games Group]] (which later became [[LucasArts]]) cut a deal with the video game company [[Wikipedia:Atari|Atri]] to produce two non-Star Wars games called [[Wikipedia:Ballblazer|''Ballblazer'']] (1984) and [[Wikipedia:Rescue on Fractalus!|''Rescue on Fractalus!'']]. After Atari encountered financial troubles, Lucasfilm entered into an agreement with video game publisher [[Wikipedia:Epyx|Epyx]] to produce two games in 1985. In February 1986, Lucas sold Lucasfilm's computer graphics group and their Pixar computer to [[Wikipedia:Apple, Inc.|Apple]] co-founder [[Wikipedia:Steve Jobs|Steve Jobs]], who turned the division into a profitable animated film studio called [[Wikipedia:Pixar|Pixar]].<ref name="Jones" />

In 1987, [[Howard Roffman]], the new Vice President of Lucas Licensing, was tasked with reviving Lucasfilm's toy line, which had dropped to US$35 million by 1985. Under Roffman and Lucas's leadership, Lucasfilm produced and collaborated on several non-Star Wars films including [[Wikipedia:Howard the Duck (film)|''Howard the Duck'']] (1986), [[Wikipedia:Labyrinth (1986 film)|''Labyrinth'']] (1986), [[Wikipedia:Willow (film)|''Willow'']] (1988), [[Wikipedia:Tucker: The Man and His Dream|''Tucker: The Man and His Dream'']] (1988), the third ''Indiana Jones'' film [[Wikipedia:Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade|''Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade'']] (1989), the [[Wikipedia:Radioland Murders|''Radioland Murders'']] (1994), and [[Wikipedia:The Young Indiana Jones Chronicles|''The Young Indiana Jones Chronicles'']] (1992-1993).<ref name="Taylor" /><ref name="Jones" />

Besides cinematic films, Lucasfilm also worked with Disney to developed a Star Wars–themed simulated ride at their Disney parks called [[Star Tours (real-world)|''Star Tours'']] and a 3-D movie featuring popstar [[Wikipedia:Michael Jackson|Michael Jackson]] called [[Wikipedia:Captain EO|''Captain EO'']]. During the 1990s, Lucas also worked with [[Wikipedia:Apple Inc.|Apple Inc.]], [[Wikipedia:National Geographic|National Geographic]], the [[Wikipedia:Smithsonian Institution|Smithsonian Institution]], and the [[Wikipedia:National Audobon Society|National Audubon Society]] to produce several educational media including ''GTV: A Geographic Perspective on American History'', ''Life Story: The Race for the Double Helix'', and ''Mystery of the Disappearing Ducks''.<ref name="Jones" />

During the 1990s, Lucas expanded Skywalker Ranch to nearly 5,000 acres to accommodate more office space at the adjacent Big Rock Ranch. However, Lucasfilm was unable to relocate ILM from [[Wikipedia:San Rafael|San Rafael]] due to opposition from [[Wikipedia:Marin County, California|Marin County]] residents.<ref name="Jones" /> [[Hal Barwood]] also joined LucasArts in 1990. Under Barwood's leadership, the subsidiary produced several computer games including [[Wikipedia:Indiana Jones and the Fate of Atlantis|''Indiana Jones and the Fate of Atlantis'']], [[Wikipedia:Monkey Island (series)|''Monkey Island'']], and [[Wikipedia:Grim Fandango|''Grim Fandango'']].<ref name="Taylor" />

===The Expanded Universe===
[[File:HTTE-Legends.png|thumb|right|200px|Lucas allowed various authors and creators to produced licensed Star Wars works including Timothy Zahn's ''Heir to the Empire''.]]
Following the release of ''The Return of the Jedi'', Lucasfilm worked on several Star Wars– television productions including the 1984 television movie ''[[Caravan of Courage: An Ewok Adventure]]'', the 1985 sequel film ''[[Ewoks: The Battle for Endor]]'', and the cartoon series ''[[Star Wars: Droids: The Adventures of R2-D2 and C-3PO]]'' and [[Ewoks (television series)|''Ewoks'']], which were distributed by [[ABC Television Network|ABC Television]]. Produced by [[Nelvana]] animation studio, ''Droids'' and ''Ewoks'' were intended to generate a toy merchandising line. However, the two animated series did not perform well commercially with ''Ewoks'' being canceled after two seasons and ''Droids'' after one season.<ref name="Jones" />

In May 1987, Lucasfilm hosted the first Star Wars convention at a hotel near Los Angeles, which attracted 10,000 events. Lucas himself attended the event, accompanied by the Star Wars characters [[R2-D2]] and [[C-3PO]] and [[Wikipedia:Star Trek|''Star Trek'']] creator [[Wikipedia:Gene Roddenberry|Gene Roddenberry]].<ref name="Taylor" /> That same year, Lucasfilm launched a fan magazine called the ''Lucasfilm Fan Club'' magazine.<ref name="Jones" /> In October 1987, Lucasfilm also licensed Star Wars role playing games to the start-up [[West End Games]], which helped expanded the [[Star Wars Legends|Star Wars Expanded Universe]] by creating new alien species, ships, weapons, and droids. Until 1990, West End Games' Star Wars role playing games were the only Star Wars stories being produced.<ref name="Taylor" />

In 1988, [[Wikipedia:Lou Aronica|Lou Aronica]], the-then head of mass market publishing at [[Wikipedia:Bantam Books|Bantam Books]] convinced Lucas and Lucas Licensing Vice President Howard Roffman to license a series of Star Wars novels set following the events of ''Return of the Jedi''. The first of these novels was [[Timothy Zahn]]'s ''[[Heir to the Empire]]'' (1991), which became a bestseller and spawned [[Dark Force Rising|two]] [[The Last Command|sequels]]. The successful [[Star Wars: The Thrawn Trilogy|''Thrawn Trilogy'']] led to a line of Expanded Universe novels, which were produced under the [[Bantam Spectra]] imprint.<ref name="Taylor" /><ref name="Jones" />

During the 1990s, Lucasfilm entered into a licensing agreement with [[Dark Horse Comics]], beginning with the 1991 comic ''[[Star Wars: Dark Empire]]''. Beginning in 1993, the company also entered into licensing agreements with [[Wikipedia:Nintendo|Nintendo]] to produce Star Wars computer games, trading chards, and bendable figures. In 1995, [[Hasbro Inc.]] produced a new line of Star Wars toys under the imprint "The Power of the Force." In 1997, Lucasfilm released the commercially''[[The Star Wars Trilogy Special Edition]]'', which inserted digitally added characters and creatures into existing scenes.<ref name="Jones" />

In 1998, [[Del Rey]] acquired the Lucasfilm publishing license from Bantam Spectra as part of a publishing deal connected to the upcoming [[Prequel trilogy]]. The following year, Del Rey launched a new line of Star Wars Expanded Universe novels known as the [[Star Wars: The New Jedi Order|''New Jedi Order'']] series, which introduced the extragalactic [[Yuuzhan Vong]] as antagonists.<ref name="Taylor" />

By the early 21st century, the Expanded Universe had spawned numerous licensed novels, comics, and computer games including the [[Star Wars: Tales of the Jedi (comic series)|''Tales of the Jedi'']] comics, [[BioWare]]'s ''[[Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic]]'' computer game, and the online role playing game ''[[Star Wars: The Old Republic]]''. To maintain consistency between the various stories, Lucasfilm created an internal database known as the [[Holocron continuity database]], which lists the various Star Wars characters, planets, ships, and concepts. This database is still maintained by Lucasfilm employee [[Leland Chee]].<ref name="Taylor" />

===The Prequels and beyond===
[[File:Letterman_Digital_Arts_Center.jpg|thumb|left|250px|Lucasfilm's present headquarters, the Letterman Digital Arts Center]]
Lucas began work on the script for [[Star Wars: Episode I The Phantom Menace|''Star Wars'': Episode I ''The Phantom Menace'']] on [[November 1]], [[1994]]. The film premiered on [[May 19]], [[1999]], grossing a total of US$926 million by the end of the year. The film's sequel [[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones|''Star Wars'': Episode II ''Attack of the Clones'']] was released on [[May 16]], [[2002]], grossing US$649 million by the end of the year. The third film [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith|''Star Wars'': Episode III ''Revenge of the Sith'']] was released on [[May 16]], [[2005]], grossing US$848 million worldwide. The prequel trilogy grossed $2.4 billion at the box office, with much of the revenue going back to Lucasfilm.<ref name="Jones" />

Following the release of ''Attack of the Clones'', Lucasfilm sold off its theatre sound division THX as a separate private company in which Lucas held a minority stake. Lucas also moved most of the company's divisions into Red Rock Ranch since they had outgrown the Skywalker Ranch premises. By that stage, ILM had grown to around 1,500 employees.<ref name="Taylor" />

In June 1999, Lucas won a bid to build a [[Letterman Digital Arts Center|new digital complex]] on the site of the former [[Wikipedia: Letterman Army Hospital|Letterman Army Medical Center]] at the [[Wikipedia:Presidio of San Francisco|Presidio]] in San Francisco. Lucas intended for this new complex to house ILM, THX, LucasArts, and the headquarters of Lucasfilm. The deal with the Presidio Trust was signed in August 2001, with construction beginning on [[February 8]], [[2003]]. In June [[2005]], Lucasfilm's marketing, online, and licensing units moved into the new Letterman Digital Arts Center. The new Letterman complex also housed Lucasfilm's subsidiaries ILM, THX, LucasArts, and the George Lucas Educational Foundation.<ref name="Jones" />

Following the completion of the Prequel Trilogy, Lucas envisioned Lucasfilm as a "widget-driven" company. While it would continue producing books, video games, music, and special effects, film production would be scaled back apart from the next ''Indiana Jones'' film. ILM, THX, and Skywalker Ranch were the most productive components of the company, generating most of their revenue from non-Star Wars projects. ILM worked on special effects for several successful film franchises including [[Wikipedia:Iron Man (2008 film)|''Iron Man'']], [[Wikipedia:Harry Potter (film series)|''Harry Potter'']], [[Wikipedia:Pirates of the Caribbean|''Pirates of the Caribbean'']], and [[Wikipedia:Avatar (2009 film)|''Avatar'']]. Meanwhile, [[Skywalker Sound]] worked on the sound of nearly 40 films and television series.<ref name="Jones" />

Inspired by the success of former Lucasfilm subsidiary Pixar, Lucas established a computer animation division called [[Lucasfilm Animation]], which was based at Big Rock Ranch.<ref name="Jones" /> In 2005, Lucasfilm Animation established a [[Lucasfilm Animation Singapore|branch]] in [[Wikipedia:Singapore|Singapore]] at the "Sandcrawler Building," giving the company an international presence.<ref name="Today Online">{{WebCite|url=https://www.todayonline.com/singapore/pm-lee-opens-lucasfilms-sandcrawler-building|text=PM Lee opens Lucasfilm's Sandcrawler building|author=Tan Weizhen|date=2014-01-17|work=[[Wikipedia:Today (website)|''Today'']]|archivedate=20210308144034}}</ref>

However, LucasArts was unprofitable since it had marketed its products at the [[Wikipedia:Player computer|PC game]] market and was unable to come up with its own original game concepts.<ref name="Jones" /> The division had also focused more on building software engines than on the games themselves. Following an internal audit in 2004, half of LucasArt's staff were laid off. In 2010, another third of the division's staff were laid off following the dismissal of the LucasArt's chief [[Jim Ward (Lucasfilm)|Jim Ward]]. Some of LucasArts' notable projects included the canceled computer game ''[[Star Wars: 1313]]'', the canceled live action TV show ''[[Star Wars: Underworld]]'', the [[Star Wars Battlefront (series)|''Star Wars Battlefront'']] computer game series, and several ''[[LEGO Star Wars]]'' computer games.<ref name="Taylor" />

Following the release of ''Revenge of the Sith'', Lucas and director, writer, and animator [[Dave Filoni]] produced a 3D computer animated series called ''[[Star Wars: The Clone Wars]]''. The series was distributed by [[Cartoon Network]], with the first episodes being combined into an animated movie called [[Star Wars: The Clone Wars (film)|''The Clone Wars'']]. The first season of the ''Clone Wars'' aired on Cartoon Network on [[October 3]], [[2008]]. Due to its success, the series was renewed for another five seasons.<ref name="Jones" />

Beside Star Wars, Lucasfilm also produced several non-Star Wars films including [[Wikipedia:Indiana Jones and the Kingdom of the Crystal Skulls|''Indiana Jones and the Kingdom of the Crystal Skulls'']] (2008), World War II film [[Wikipedia:Red Tails|''Red Tails'']] (2012), and animated fantasy [[Wikipedia:Strange Magic (film)|''Strange Magic'']] (2015). These films received mixed reviews and did not perform well commercially. The critical response to ''Red Tails'' upset Lucas and let him to retire from filmmaking.<ref name="Jones" />

===Disney acquisition===
[[File:DisneyLucas.jpg|thumb|right|250px|George Lucas and Bob Iger finalizing the deal to sell Lucasfilm to Disney in October 2012.]]
In May 2011, George Lucas met with [[The Walt Disney Company|Disney]] CEO [[Bob Iger (chief executive officer)|Bob Iger]] at [[Wikipedia:Walt Disney World|Walt Disney World]] to discuss selling Lucasfilm. Following the visit, Lucas recruited [[Kathleen Kennedy]], former President of the [[Wikipedia:Producers Guild of America|Producers Guild of America]], as his successor. Lucas and Kennedy started work on the [[Sequel trilogy]] and hired [[Michael Arndt]] as a screenwriter for what became [[Star Wars: Episode VII The Force Awakens|''Star Wars'': Episode VII ''The Force Awakens'']].<ref name="Jones" /><ref name="Taylor" /> In June 2012, Kennedy assumed the position of co-chair of Lucasfilm.<ref name="Insider 135">{{InsiderCite|135|Launchpad|Launch Pad}}</ref>

Following Kennedy's promotion, lawyers and accountants scrutinized Lucasfilm's intellectual property in July and August 2012 to ensure that Lucas owned the rights to the characters of the Star Wars universe.<ref name="Taylor" /> After negotiations between Lucas and Iger, Lucas agreed to sell Lucasfilm in return for Disney agreeing to use his story treatments for the Sequel Trilogy in future stories. According to Iger, this was done on the understanding that Disney would not be contractually obligated to adhere to Lucas' plot lines. In addition, Iger also agreed to give Lucasfilm the same kind of limited autonomy enjoyed by Disney's other subsidiaries Pixar and Marvel. On [[October 30]], 2012, Lucas and Iger signed an agreement selling Lucasfilm to Disney for for $4.05 billion in stock and cash.<ref name="Jones" /><ref name="Taylor" /><ref name="Iger">{{BookCite|author=[[Bob Iger (chief executive officer)|Robert Iger]]|title=The Ride of a Lifetime: Lessons Learned from 15 Years as CEO of the Walt Disney Company|publisher=[[Random House]]|date=2019|isbn=978-0399592096}}</ref><ref name="Disney acquires Lucasfilm">{{DisneyCompany|url=disney-news/press-releases/2012/10/disney-acquire-lucasfilm-ltd|text=DISNEY TO ACQUIRE LUCASFILM LTD.}}</ref> On [[December 21]], 2012, The Walt Disney Company officially finalized its purchase of Lucasfilm.<ref name="Disney completes Lucasfilm acquisition>{{WebCite|url=http://insidemovies.ew.com/2012/12/21/walt-disney-completes-lucasfilm-acquisition|text=Mickey meets 'Star Wars': Walt Disney Co. completes acquisition of Lucasfilm|author=Solvej Schou|date=2012-12-21|work=''[[Entertainment Weekly]]''|archivedate=20121222151818}}</ref>

===Sequel Trilogy and Star Wars Stories era===
====Films and TV shows====
Following the Disney acquisition, Lucasfilm underwent some restructuring and reorientation. At Kathleen Kennedy's initiative, the [[Lucasfilm Story Group]] was set up in 2012 under the leadership of [[Kiri Hart]] to coordinate the production of new Star Wars films and stories in the absence of Lucas.<ref name="Taylor" /><ref name="ForceCast">{{WebCite|url=http://www.forcecast.net/story/home/ForceCast_273_The_Galaxy_Is_Reading_154431.asp|text=ForceCast #273: The Galaxy is Reading|work=[[The ForceCast]]|archivedate=20200412073227}}</ref> In 2013, Lucasfilm canceled ''The Clone Wars'' television series. Kennedy however retained the show's animation supervisor Filoni and his team, who produced a new animated television series called ''[[Star Wars Rebels]]''.<ref name="Taylor" /> The series ran for four seasons between 2014 and 2018 and was nominated for four [[Wikipedia:Emmy Awards|Emmy Awards]].<ref name="SW Rebels fourth season">{{SW|url=news/star-wars-rebels-to-return-for-fourth-season-this-fall|text=''Star Wars Rebels'' to Return for Fourth Season This Fall}}</ref><ref name="Emmys Rebels">{{WebCite|url=https://www.emmys.com/shows/star-wars-rebels|text=Star Wars Rebels AWARDS & NOMINATIONS|work=[[Wikipedia:Emmy Awards|Emmy Awards]]|archivedate=20190904082728}}</ref>

In March 2014, Lucasfilm confirmed that ''Episode VII'' would be set 30 years after the events of ''Return of the Jedi''. In addition to three new leads, Lucasfilm also confirmed that several Original Trilogy stars including [[Mark Hamill]], [[Carrie Fisher]], [[Harrison Ford]], [[Anthony Daniels]], [[Kenny Baker]], and [[Peter Mayhew]] would reprise their roles. [[J.J. Abrams]] was recruited as director while [[Lawrence Kasdan]] replaced Arndt as screenwriter.<ref name="Taylor" />

''The Force Awakens'' was released on December 2015, grossing US$2 billion worldwide.<ref name="Taylor" /> Following the release of ''The Force Awakens'', Lucasfilm produced four Star Wars cinematic films: ''[[Rogue One: A Star Wars Story]]'' (2016),<ref name="Empire Online Rogue One">{{WebCite|url=https://www.empireonline.com/movies/rogue-one-star-wars-story/rogue-one-switches-uk-release-date-15-december|text=Rogue One switches its UK release date to 15 December|author=James White|date=2016-10-24|work=[[Wikipedia:Empire (magazine)|''Empire'']]|archivedate=20190104055211}}</ref> [[Star Wars: Episode VIII The Last Jedi|''Star Wars'': Episode VIII ''The Last Jedi'']] (2017),<ref name="Episode VIII release date">{{SW|url=news/star-wars-episode-viii-to-open-december-15-2017|text=''Star Wars: Episode VIII'' to Open December 15, 2017}}</ref> ''[[Solo: A Star Wars Story]]'' (2018), and [[Star Wars: Episode IX The Rise of Skywalker|''Star Wars'': Episode IX ''The Rise of Skywalker'']] (2019).<ref name="Hollywood Reporter ====Disney+ Era====
[[File:Lucasfilm-future-2020.png|thumb|left|250px|Kathleen Kennedy unveiling new Star Wars projects at the 2020 Disney Investor Day.]]
In October [[2018]], Lucasfilm also commenced production on a live-action Star Wars television series called ''[[Star Wars: The Mandalorian]]''. [[Jon Favreau]] served as the series' showrunner with directors including Filoni, [[Deborah Chow]], [[Rick Famuyiwa]], [[Bryce Dallas Howard]], and [[Taika Waititi]].<ref name="Production begins">{{SW|url=news/the-mandalorian-revealed|text=''The Mandalorian'' First Image, Directors Revealed}}</ref> Airing on [[Disney+]] on [[November 12]], [[2019]], the series was well-received by both critics and fans.<ref name="Observer Mandalorian">{{WebCite|url=https://observer.com/2019/12/the-mandalorian-episode-5-disney-plus-ratings-viewership|text='The Mandalorian' Is Earning Global Demand Even as Disney+ Slow-Plays Its Rollout|author=Brandon Katz|date=2019-12-03|work=[[Wikipedia:The New York Observer|''The New York Observer'']]|archivedate=20200121010526}}</ref> It was renewed for a [[The Mandalorian Season Two|second season]], which aired on Disney+ on [[October 30]], [[2020]].<ref name="Mandalorian Season Two">{{SW|url=news/the-mandalorian-season-two-october-30|text=''The Mandalorian'' Season Two Begins October 30 on Disney+}}</ref>

In December 2020, Kennedy confirmed that Lucasfilm was working on at least ten Star Wars films and television shows including [[Patty Jenkins]]' [[Rogue Squadron (film)|''Rogue Squadron'']], an untitled Taika Waititi film, several Disney+ webseries and films including [[Star Wars: Obi-Wan Kenobi|''Obi-Wan Kenobi'']], [[Star Wars: Ahsoka|''Ahsoka'']], ''[[Rangers of the New Republic]]'', a [[Landonis Balthazar Calrissian|Lando Calrissian]] series, ''[[Star Wars: Andor]]'', ''[[Star Wars: The Acolyte]]'', the animated ''[[Star Wars: The Bad Batch]]'' series, the anime ''[[Star Wars: Visions]]'', and ''[[A Droid Story]]''. In addition, Kennedy confirmed that the company was also working on at least two non-Star Wars projects including a sequel to the 1988 fantasy film [[Wikipedia:Willow (film)|''Willow'']] and an adaptation of [[Wikipedia:Tomi Adeyemi|Tomi Adeyemi's]] young adult novel [[Wikipedia:Children of Blood and Bone|''Children of Blood and Bone'']].<ref name="Future Lucasfilm projects">{{SW|url=news/future-lucasfilm-projects-revealed|text=Future Lucasfilm Projects Revealed}}</ref>

In September 2022, Lucasfilm confirmed that it was working on several new productions including the animated series [[Star Wars: Tales of the Jedi (television series)|''Star Wars: Tales of the Jedi'']], ''[[Star Wars: Young Jedi Adventures]]'', and the live-action series ''[[Star Wars: Skeleton Crew]]''. In addition, the company confirmed the release dates for its [[Wikipedia:Willow (TV series)|''Willow'']] TV series and a fifth [[Wikipedia:Indiana Jones|''Indiana Jones'']] film.<ref name="D23 EXPO 2022">{{SW|url=news/d23-expo-2022-lucasfilm|text=D23 Expo 2022: All the Big Lucasfilm Reveals for ''Andor'', ''Willow'', ''The Mandalorian'', and More}}</ref>

In April 2023, Kennedy confirmed at [[Celebration Europe (2023)|Celebration Europe]] that the studio was working on three live-action films directed by [[James Mangold]], Dave Filoni, and [[Sharmeen Obaid-Chinoy]]. Mangold's film will explore the dawn of the Jedi Order while Filoni's film will be set in the New Republic era and connect several Disney+ shows including ''The Mandalorian'', ''[[Star Wars: The Book of Boba Fett]]'', and ''Ahsoka''. Obaid-Chinoy's film will be set after the events of ''The Rise of Skywalker'' with [[Daisy Ridley]] reprising her role as [[Rey Skywalker]].<ref name="SWCE 2023 movies">{{SW|url=news/swce-2023-new-star-wars-films|text=SWCE 2023: Three New ''Star Wars'' Movies Announced}}</ref> In addition, Lucasfilm confirmed the release dates for ''Ahsoka'' and ''The Acolyte'' in August 2023 and 2024 respectively.<ref name="USA Today SWCE">{{WebCite|url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/entertainment/movies/2023/04/07/star-wars-celebration-2023-daisy-ridley-new-movies/11560731002|text=Star Wars Celebration: New movie will bring back Daisy Ridley as Rey, three films planned|author=Nicole Fallert|date=2023-04-07|work=[[Wikipedia:USA Today|''USA Today'']]|archivedate=20230408093755}}</ref>

====Licensed literature and media====
[[File:Star_Wars_The_High_Republic_Announcement_Logo.jpg|thumb|right|250px|''The High Republic'' multimedia publishing project]]
In February 2013, the LucasArts division was dissolved, leading to the layoff of 200 employees. Ten employees remained to coordinate the production of licensed Star Wars computer games.<ref name="WSJ LucasArts">{{WebCite|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/SB10001424127887323916304578400962825215302|text=Disney to Shut LucasArts Videogame Unit|author=Ben Fritz|date=2013-04-03|work=[[Wikipedia:The Wall Street Journal|''The Wall Street Journal'']]|archivedate=20150606074724}}</ref>

In May 2013, Lucasfilm and [[Disney Interactive]] signed a multi-year, multi-title exclusive contract with [[Electronic Arts]] to produce Star Wars computer games. While EA would focus on high quality products, Disney Interactive retained the rights to produce games for more "casual audiences".<ref name="EA">{{SW|url=news/electronic-arts-selected-for-multi-year-agreement-for-the-future-of-star-wars-gaming|text=Electronic Arts Selected for Multi-Year Agreement for the Future of Star Wars Gaming}}</ref> Notable Star Wars games published under the EA license have included ''[[Star Wars Battlefront]]'' (2015),<ref name="EA Battlefront">{{WebCite|url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/davidthier/2013/06/10/ea-announces-new-star-wars-battlegront-game|text=EA Announces New Star Wars: Battlefront Game|author=Dave Thier|date=2013-06-10|work=[[Wikipedia:Forbes|''Forbes'']]|archivedate=20140328181007}}</ref>''[[Star Wars Battlefront II]]'' (2017),<ref name="Future of EA">{{EA|url=news/the-future-of-ea-and-star-wars-games|text=The Future of EA Star Wars™ Games|author=Jade Raymond}}</ref> ''[[Star Wars Jedi: Fallen Order]]'',<ref name="Fallen Order">{{SW|url=games-apps/star-wars-jedi-fallen-order|text=Star Wars Jedi: Fallen Order}}</ref> and ''[[Star Wars: Squadrons]]'' (2020).<ref name="SW Squadrons">{{SW|url=news/star-wars-squadrons|text=''Star Wars: Squadrons'', a New Space Combat Game, Coming October 2}}</ref>

In January 2014, Lucasfilm granted [[Marvel Comics]] the exclusive rights to produce new Star Wars comics from 2015.<ref name="Marvel 2014">{{SW|url=news/lucasfilm-and-marvel-join-forces-to-publish-star-wars-comics-and-graphic-novels|text=Disney's Lucasfilm and Marvel Entertainment Join Forces to Publish ''Star Wars'' Comics and Graphic Novels|nolive=1}}</ref> On [[April 25]], 2014, the Lucasfilm Story Group clarified that only Lucas' six Star Wars films and ''The Clone Wars'' television series were [[Canon]]. The Expanded Universe was reclassified as [[Star Wars Legends]] and would be used as resources for future stories. All subsequent Star Wars literature and media would be part of a single, cohesive continuity.<ref name="Taylor" /><ref name="Legendary EU">{{SW|url=news/the-legendary-star-wars-expanded-universe-turns-a-new-page|text=The Legendary Star Wars Expanded Universe Turns a New Page}}</ref> Shortly after Lucasfilm's Canon reboot, Disney Publishing Worldwide announced that Del Rey would be publishing a new line of Canon Star Wars novels on a bi-monthly schedule, commencing with [[John Jackson Miller]]'s 2014 novel ''[[A New Dawn]]''.<ref name="Hollywood Reporter Del Rey">{{WebCite|url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/heat-vision/disney-del-rey-launch-new-698995|text=Disney, Del Rey to Launch New 'Star Wars' Book Line|author=Graeme Macmillan|date=2014-04-25|work=[[Wikipedia:The Hollywood Reporter|''The Hollywood Reporter'']]|archivedate=20201129143307}}</ref>

In April 2017, [[IDW Publishing]] acquired a license from Lucasfilm to produce all-ages Star Wars comics, commencing with [[Star Wars Adventures (2017)|''Star Wars Adventures'']].<ref name="Comics Beat IDW">{{WebCite|url=https://www.comicsbeat.com/star-wars-publishing-round-up-idws-star-wars-comics-for-kids-and-captain-phasmas-own-book|text=Star Wars publishing round-up: IDW's Star Wars comics for kids and Captain Phasma's own book|author=Heidi MacDonald|date=2017-04-17|work=[https://www.comicsbeat.com ''The Beat'']|archivedate=20201204173601}}</ref>

In February 2020, Lucasfilm unveiled a new multimedia publishing project called ''[[Star Wars: The High Republic]]'', which is set 200 years before the events of ''The Phantom Menace''. Key authors associated with the project included [[Claudia Gray]], [[Daniel José Older]], [[Charles Soule]], [[Cavan Scott]], and [[Justina Ireland]].<ref name="5 Things High Republic">{{SW|url=news/5-things-we-learned-about-star-wars-the-high-republic|text=5 Things We Learned About ''Star Wars: The High Republic''}}</ref> ''The High Republic'' involved several publishers, including Del Rey, [[Disney–Lucasfilm Press]], IDW Publishing, and Marvel Comics.<ref name="NYCC Project Luminous">{{SW|url=news/nycc-2019-project-luminous-details-and-more-revealed-at-the-lucasfilm-publishing-panel|text=NYCC 2019: 'Project Luminous' Details and More Revealed at the Lucasfilm Publishing Panel}}</ref>

In January 2021, Lucasfilm revealed a new [[Star Wars video games|video game]] division called [[Lucasfilm Games]] to provide support to the company's licensed publishers and developers.<ref name="New Era">{{SW|url=news/lucasfilm-games|text=Lucasfilm Games Begins a New Era}}</ref> That same month, Lucasfilm Games entered into partnerships with publishers [[Ubisoft]] and [[Wikipedia:Bethesda Softworks|Bethesda Softworks]] to produce new Star Wars and ''Indian Jones'' computer games.<ref name="New Partnerships">{{WebCite|url=https://www.wired.com/story/lucasfilm-games-star-wars-ubisoft-indiana-jones-bethesda|text=Lucasfilm Games' New Partnerships Mean the Galaxy's the Limit|author=Eric Ravenscraft|date=2021-01-13|work=[[Wikipedia:Wired (magazine)|''Wired'']]|archivedate=20210410044112}}</ref> In February 2021, Lucasfilm Games confirmed that it was working with games developer [[Zynga]] to produce a competitive arena combat game called ''[[Star Wars: Hunters]]''.<ref name="Hunters">{{SW|url=news/star-wars-hunters-game-announce|text=Join the Fight in ''Star Wars: Hunters'', a New Game from Lucasfilm and Zynga}}</ref>

In November 2021, Dark Horse Comics announced that it would be collaborating with Lucasfilm and Disney Publishing Worldwide to produce a new line of all-age comics and graphic novels stretching from the ''High Republic'' to the [[First Order]] era.<ref name="Return">{{SW|url=news/dark-horse-comics-announce|text=Dark Horse Comics Returns to the ''Star Wars'' Galaxy}}</ref>

==Organizational structure==
Lucasfilm was reorganized to consolidate all the companies to be under Lucasfilm. Today, Lucasfilm is made up of six divisions:
*Lucasfilm – film and television production and promotion
*[[Lucas Digital]] – company composed of [[Industrial Light & Magic]] (ILM) and [[Skywalker Sound]]. This company may not exist any longer since the Walt Disney Company reorganization.
**Industrial Light & Magic – digital and visual effects for the entertainment industry
**Skywalker Sound – post-production sound editing for the entertainment industry
**[[ILM Immersive]] – virtual reality, mixed reality, real-time cinema, theme-park entertainment, and narrative-based entertainment for future platforms
*[[Lucasfilm Animation]] and [[Lucasfilm Animation Singapore]] – digital animation studio for film, television, and games
*[[Lucasfilm Franchise Animation]] – ancillary animation studio for television and digital content partnered with outside studios
*[[Lucasfilm Games]] is a division of Lucasfilm dedicated to providing support to partners [[Star Wars video games|video game]] publishers and developers.
*[[Lucas Licensing]] – licensing and merchandising for ''Star Wars'', ''Indiana Jones'', and other Lucasfilm projects
**[[Lucas Learning]] – producing technology-based educational materials for K–12. Its official site now leads to the nonprofit George Lucas Educational Foundation's Edutopia.
**[[LucasBooks]] – the publishing arm of Lucasfilm and a part of Lucas Licensing
*[[Lucas Online]] – online destination for entertainment, reference, education, and e-commerce for Lucasfilm projects

===Former divisions===
A few other companies started out under the Lucasfilm umbrella:
*[[LucasArts]] – developer and publisher of interactive entertainment software. The company was eventually reorganized in a licensing model following the Walt Disney Company's acquisition of Lucasfilm.
*[[Black Falcon Ltd.|Black Falcon]] – handled merchandising for Lucasfilm briefly before fully merging into Lucasfilm in December 1979
*[[Wikipedia:Pixar|Pixar]] – computer animation film-production company (sold to [[Wikipedia:Steve Jobs|Steve Jobs]] in 1986)
*[[THX]] – theater sound system (spun off from Lucasfilm in 2002)
*[[Star Wars Corporation]] – started separately in 1973 and absorbed into Lucasfilm by 1980
